observations.recommendation:
1.5. We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Ensure that all transactions involving the recognition of expenses
against inventory held for consumption are properly supported by
42
SSMI with a copy of the issued RIS, confirming that all supplies issued
are documented;
b. Cease the practice of derecognizing accounts within the inventory held
for distribution when no actual distribution has taken place, as this will
result in inaccuracies and misstatements in the financial reports;
c. Ensure that the distribution of welfare goods procured for stockpiling
is backed by a declaration of a state of calamity, along with
acknowledgment receipts, photographs, or other equivalent proof of
distribution;
d. Require the Municipal Agriculturist to submit the original copy of the
post-master list of recipients to ensure that agricultural supplies are
distributed to eligible farmer-beneficiaries;
e. Correct the records in the books to classify the 40 solar water pumps as
assets of the LGU, as they have not been distributed yet. However, after
these pumps are distributed from the storage area, the Municipal
Agriculturist should provide the following documents:
1) A post-master list of recipients for solar water pumps, along with
a supporting list of qualifications to ensure equitable distribution; and
2) MOA to serve as a formal document outlining the terms,
responsibilities, and mutual understanding between the LGU and
the beneficiaries
f. Ensure that financial transactions are recorded using the appropriate
account titles prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009, which
outlines the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs, for proper
presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

3.3 To address the foregoing issues, we have recommended that Management:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the designated Property/Supply
Officer to ensure that all semi-expendable properties recognized under
the Office Supplies Expenses and Other Supplies and Materials
Expenses accounts, amounting to ₱604,215.00 and ₱348,420.00,
respectively, are duly supported with ICS, and properly recorded and
reported using the prescribed forms. Adjusting entries are no longer
necessary because these were already recognized as expenses, which
have already been closed to the Retained Earnings account.
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all semi-expendable
properties acquired and issued before CY 2024 with a total carrying
amount of ₱2,506,011.06, are properly adjusted/corrected in the books
of accounts in accordance with Annex C of COA Circular 2024-006,
which provides illustrative accounting entries to ensure proper
recording, adjustments, and reclassification of accounts relative to the
increase of the capitalization threshold of PPEs.
c. Require the Municipal Accountant and the Property/Supply Officer to
use the forms, registries, and reports prescribed under Item 4.7 of COA
Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022, as may be deemed
applicable, to ensure that semi-expendable properties acquired and
issued in CY 2024 onwards and in the prior years are properly
controlled and accounted for.

3.6. We have recommended that Management require the Municipal
Accountant to verify the validity of payables aged two years and more, send
confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors, and
remunerate all valid claims. Otherwise, cause the reversion of the payables
to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund.

4.4. We have reiterated the audit recommendation from the prior year that the
Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to provide
adequate disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements, as previously
mentioned, to ensure that the accounts are presented fairly in the financial
statements and that users of financial information are properly guided in
their decision-making.

We recommended that Management facilitate fair presentation of accounts by: a. instructing the Inventory Committee to conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE items, including those currently in progress; and b. directing both the GSO and Accounting Office to continuously reconcile the results of the physical count of PPE with the balances of PPE in the accounting records, especially the last unreconciled beginning balances of PPEs acquired before CY 2009 that lack PCs and PPELCs; and if identification efforts prove unfeasible, considering the applicability of the guidelines established under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 on the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts.

We recommended the Municipal Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the subject PPE items to their appropriate specific PPE accounts and the corresponding accumulated depreciation accounts to ensure transparency of the accounts presented and the fair presentation of the account balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended the Municipal Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to recompute the depreciation of Road Networks in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-008 by not providing salvage value to the respective assets and effect the necessary adjusting entry to accurately restate the net book value of the assets.

We recommended that Management: a. intensify efforts in collecting dues from delinquent clients of the Alcala Municipal Water System by continuously sending demand letters to ensure timely payment; b. exert diligent efforts to locate all necessary supporting documents essential for issuing the demand letter to the concerned contractor; c. reissue at least three (3) sequential demand letters to the officials of Alcala Tobacco Farmers Cooperative, compelling them to submit the Utilization or Liquidation Report for the P6,000,000.00 financial assistance granted to them; and d. exhaust all available legal remedies to collect the balance of the unreturned properties and cash shortage of the accountable officers.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and record the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to reclassify the amount recorded under Due to Other Payables to Due to Officers and Employees in line with Section 113 of PD1445 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that Management: a. exert best effort to locate pertinent documents to substantiate and assess the collectability of the dormant accounts; and b. if collection is no longer feasible, undertake the appropriate action for the proper disposition of these accounts, following the procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

We recommended that Management: a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to exert all efforts to trace the details of the unidentified balances in the Inter-Agency Payables account and immediately remit the same to the concerned agencies or prepare the appropriate adjusting entries, as the case maybe; and b. ensure that all statutory deductions from employees’ salaries are promptly and accurately remitted to the concerned government agencies within the prescribed periods to avoid the imposition of penalties, surcharges, or interests, and to ensure the employees’ service records and benefits are not adversely affected.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund by accelerating the implementation of pending and reprogrammed projects, identifying the root causes of the delays and non-implementation, and developing practical, targeted, solutions to ensure the effective execution of developmental initiatives.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, investigate the source of the excess cash back up, and revert the same to the proper books to ensure that the Trust Fund reflects only actual unutilized balances and that the surplus is made available for other social services identified by the local Sanggunian in pursuance with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we hereby encourage the LGU to use geo-tagging technology to facilitate the documentation of various project sites to take photos in all stages of implementation, which is more reliable.  Moreover, the adoption of geo-tagging as one of the best practices in the validation and monitoring of projects has the following benefits and advantages, to wit:

It is cost-efficient and safe.  It enables the project monitoring team to validate, monitor, and evaluate actual progress on the ground without traveling to hard-to-access locations and areas with high-security risks, hence saving manpower resources, time, and effort.

For easy reference. It provides donors, implementing agencies, contractors and technical audit specialists with useful information about its sub-projects such as exact locations, dates of operation, land areas and distance covered.

For transparency.  The GPS data compiled in the system assigns a unique identification tag for each project to avoid duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects, thus eliminating fictitious projects and inaccurate reporting of data.

We have recommended that Management consider the introduction of geo-tagging of projects as a best practice adopted by the LGU due to its effectiveness in providing a more transparent, accurate and reliable report of project accomplishments and further as a deterrent to possible duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books for the following:

Recognize the Construction in Progress account while the market stalls are still under construction, and upon completion of the project, the Construction in Progress account shall be reclassified under the Market account for proper identification of PPEs in the books.

Derecognize in the books any provision for depreciation of the Market Stalls, which is still under construction.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misstated balances of the Building and Other Structures account and the Repairs and Maintenance-Building and Other Structures account in the financial statements. Henceforth, ensure that the cost of major repairs, rehabilitation, improvement, and construction of infrastructure assets are capitalized or recognized as property, plant, and equipment pursuant to IPSAS 17.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and other Municipal Officials concerned to find alternative documents to support the derecognition. These may include but are not limited to, sworn statements of officials or former officials who were privy to the transactions, particularly the dispersal and investment of funds.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the Municipal Accountant to adopt the accrual accounting method to recognize income.

Require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Market Supervisor, to provide the Municipal Accountant with the Certified List of Delinquent Stall Holders as the basis for the recognition of accrued income in the books of accounts.

Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare a journal entry voucher to take up the unrecorded Accounts Receivable and the Receipts from Market Operations accounts from uncollected stall rentals pursuant to Paragraph 9 of IPSAS.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Department Heads concerned, to provide the necessary data for adequate disclosure of accounts in the Notes to the Financial Statements. This will ensure a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements, thus enabling users of financial information to make well-informed decisions.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the Office of the Municipal Engineer to prepare design plans and program of works that will render completed phases of the project “usable and structurally sound.”

Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee, in coordination with the Technical Working Group, to ensure that the LGU's design plans and program of works for projects implemented in phases are appropriately reviewed and abide by the instructions outlined in Annex A of the IRR. Key review points should include a clear delineation of work for each phase and the usability and structural soundness of the projects completed in phases.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to adhere to the provisions of Executive Order No. 77 in processing the claims for the payment of travel expenses, wherein the allowable daily travel expenses shall only be limited to the actual fare and authorized meal component of DTE, which is ₱135.00 per day for official travels within the 50-kilometer radius.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the disbursement vouchers and all documentary requirements on the payment of honoraria as provided under COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, to substantiate the validity, correctness and propriety of the claims.

Require the Municipal Budget Officer to submit a copy of the approved supplemental budget and Sangguniang Bayan resolution authorizing the use of savings from the General Fund to support the legality of the funding source on the payment of said honoraria.

Due to the above audit observations, we recommended that Management:

Refund the amount of ₱40,248.00 for incurring an expenditure that is considered irregular, unnecessary, and extravagant pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003. 

Exercise prudence in the utilization of government funds to maximize its use and attain the purpose for which it was allocated.

Adhere to specific laws, rules and regulations in the disbursement of government funds to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that Management, in collaboration with the Sanggunian Bayan, take proactive steps to expedite the review and approval of the revised revenue code. This measure is essential for the LGU to generate new and additional revenue sources to support its programs, projects, and activities, which will ultimately benefit the constituents of the Municipality of Alcala.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt the ATM or digitized payroll system in the disbursement of payroll for salaries and wages of the contractuals and JO workers of the LGU. The adoption of a digitized payroll system is highly encouraged because it can improve the cash management practices of the municipality and thereby lessen the risks of possible loss of government funds through misappropriation, theft, or robbery.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that the Head of the Agency require the BAC Chairperson through the BAC Secretariat to:

Prepare all the documents required under each stage of the procurement on time to ensure that all documents are completed at the end of the procurement process.

Submit copies of perfected contracts, approved POs, and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period of five (5) working days pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 for a timely review and evaluation thereof.

We have recommended that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to take immediate action to address the noted deficiencies and ensure that all lacking documents identified in the auditorial review, as discussed in the Audit Observation Memorandum, are submitted to ensure that the procurement activities conducted can be substantiated to be in accordance with rules, laws and regulations, thus, projects implemented are valid, legal and proper.

In view of the foregoing, we hereby encourage the LGU to use geo-tagging technology to facilitate the documentation of various project sites to take photos in all stages of implementation, which is more reliable.  Moreover, the adoption of geo-tagging as one of the best practices in the validation and monitoring of projects has the following benefits and advantages, to wit:

It is cost-efficient and safe.  It enables the project monitoring team to validate, monitor, and evaluate actual progress on the ground without traveling to hard-to-access locations and areas with high-security risks, hence saving manpower resources, time, and effort.

For easy reference. It provides donors, implementing agencies, contractors and technical audit specialists with useful information about its sub-projects such as exact locations, dates of operation, land areas and distance covered.

For transparency.  The GPS data compiled in the system assigns a unique identification tag for each project to avoid duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects, thus eliminating fictitious projects and inaccurate reporting of data.

We have recommended that Management consider the introduction of geo-tagging of projects as a best practice adopted by the LGU due to its effectiveness in providing a more transparent, accurate and reliable report of project accomplishments and further as a deterrent to possible duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects.

We have recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to undertake the following courses of action, to wit:

Ensure that no disbursement voucher shall be paid unless properly documented and signed.

Ensure that all financial transaction documents from the Treasury Office are promptly turned over to the Accounting Office for timely recording of transactions and thereby contribute to the timely submission of accounts to COA.

We have recommended that Management consider preparing an action plan where different strategies could be applied based on the age and nature of the collectibles and realistically project collections under the circumstances. The following collection strategies may be considered:

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to cause notices of delinquency to be posted at the main entrance of the Municipal Hall and in publicly accessible and conspicuous places in each barangay of the LGU concerned pursuant to Section 254 of RA 7160;

Advise the Chief of RPTS to seek assistance and intervention of barangay officials in the collection of delinquent accounts; and

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to enforce administrative and judicial actions after the expiration of the five-year collection period to all taxpayers with delinquent accounts.

We have recommended that management:

Direct the Market Supervisor to enforce the provision under the Municipal Tax Ordinance No. 2011-02 or the Market Code, which provides the cancellation or revocation of the contract of lease or award on failure to pay the rental fee for three consecutive months; and

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the Market Supervisor to formulate effective collection strategies, and ensure its implementation accordingly.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the BPLO, to: 

Implement measures to validate the declared gross receipts of businesses, especially those engaging directly with the Municipality and its barangays, to ensure the correctness of business taxes collected by the LGU.

Enforce the collection of the underpayment, including the applicable surcharges and interests provided in the Municipal Revenue Code. Non-compliance thereof may warrant the issuance of a corresponding Notice of Charge.

Require each supplier/contractor to submit audited Financial Statements (FS) and Income Tax Return (ITR) to verify the correctness of declared gross receipts to ensure the accuracy of assessment and collection of business taxes.

We have recommended that Management: 

Consider the distribution of nearly completed housing units to priority beneficiaries to ensure that the entire project does not remain idle but rather provides the less fortunate constituents of the LGU with shelter, especially the rainy season is now approaching.

Seek legal advice to ensure the release of the remaining ₱9,999,469.00 based on the binding agreement between the LGU and the NHA. 


Consider legal infractions by the contractor for not completing the project, notwithstanding the existence of a valid contract.

We have reiterated the previous year's audit recommendation that Management:

Strictly monitor the implementation of the programmed PPAs under GAD to fully maximize the utilization of the allocated funds, which should promote the attainment of the potential benefits that may be derived therefrom, such as women's empowerment and gender equality.

Refrain from charging non-gender responsive expenses against the fund allocated for GAD. Ensure that the implementation of the approved GAD Plan and Budget is in accordance with the provisions of the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA in order to address gender-related issues that will contribute to the realization of the country’s commitment, plans and policies in strengthening women empowerment, gender equality and gender development.

Recommended the Management to ensure fair physical presentation of PPE inventory. a. conducting a physical comprehensive inventory, physical inventory of all PPE items, including those currently in progress, through the Inventory Committee; b. directing both the GSO and Accounting Office to continuously reconcile the results of the physical count of PPE especially the unreconciled beginning balances acquired before CY 2009 that lack PCs and PPELCs; and c. if identification efforts prove unfeasible, considering the applicability of the guidelines established under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 on one time cleansing of PPE.

Recommended the Management to: a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to: 1) prepare adjusting journal entries to reclassify the completed projects to Road Networks account totaling to P2,767,169.86 pursuant to Section 50 of the NGAs Manual and COA Circular 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 prescribing guidelines on recording of transactions of LGUs; and 2) record the required depreciation expenses and accumulated depreciation pertaining to the reclassified Road Networks account in the amount of P21,906.77; and b. instruct the Municipal Engineer to enhance the reporting mechanisms on the status of on-going construction-in-progress and completed projects to prevent delays in recognition of the same to their appropriate accounts, both to ensure that the account balances in the financial statements are fairly presented.

Recommended that Management undertake actions to ensure settlement of receivables in favor of the LGU by issuing demand letters to the officials of Alcala Tobacco Farmers Cooperative, concerned accountable officials, former employees, and contractor, urging immediate settlement of their liabilities to the LGU to prevent loss of government fund.

Recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the accounts, Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and effect adjustments to reflect the correct account balances and present reliable data in the financial statements pursuant to Section 111(2) of PD 1445.

Recommended the Management to: a. require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Assessor’s Officer to submit the lacking documents on the purchase of lots pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure that the disbursements are valid, legal and appropriate; and b. enjoin all concerned officials not to approve the transactions and disbursements of government funds without the availability of basic documentary requirements outlined in the aforementioned Circular.

Recommended that Management: a. ensure that all identified development projects embodied in the Annual Investment Program are supported with concrete plans and designs, without any hindering issues and procurement-and-implementation-ready, including conduct of detailed engineering for infrastructure projects, in order to complete them within the target completion date to maximize the benefit for its intended beneficiaries; b. prepare a holistic and comprehensive but realistic catch-up plan, after conducting proper monitoring and follow-up activities, to expedite the project implementation of PPAs funded especially from prior years’ appropriation, which are still at the agency pipeline, to reduce backlogs; c. review the balances of the continuing appropriations as part of the annual budget preparation process and consider the need for reprogramming of the PPAs from prior years’ appropriations, where necessary, after determination of a more relevant and responsive and procurement-and-implementation-ready projects that may be included under the 20% Development Fund of the Municipality in adherence with applicable laws, rules and regulations; and d. require a certification from the Engineering Office that the detailed engineering surveys and designs have been conducted for infrastructures projects to ensure readiness of the procurement at hand, pursuant to Item 5 of Annex A of the revised IRR of RA 9184.

Recommended the Management to undertake the procedures for the proper disposal of the identified unserviceable properties in accordance with the relevant laws, rules and regulations and, after disposal of unserviceable properties, prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the disposal and derecognize the disposed properties, to gain optimal benefits for the LGU.

Recommended that a. instruct the LDRRM Officer to immediately adopt the prescribed format provided in Annex A of COA Circular 2012-002 for future LDRRMFIP in order to identify the proper classification and treatment of the expenditures and unexpended balances; and b. prepare and submit the monthly report of sources and utilization of DRRMF duly certified by the Municipal Accountant on or before the 15th day following the end of each month through the LDRRMC and LDC to the COA audit team for proper monitoring in compliance with COA Circular 2012-002.

Recommended that the BAC, through the BAC Secretariat, customize the Bidding Document to fit the requirements of the projects to be bid and ensure that there is clearly defined duties and responsibilities of contractors in order to protect the interests of the procuring entity.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management plan and conduct the one-time cleansing of the Property, Plant and Equipment account balances by adopting the guidelines and procedures  in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in order to establish reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability. From then on, the Management shall ensure that the proper accounting and reporting procedures for all acquisition/ receipt/ issue/ transfer/ disposal of PPEs are observed pursuant to the Government Accounting Manual.

We recommended that the following be observed:
The Municipal Accountant, upon receipt of the AIR and other supporting documents, such as Purchase Orders, Delivery Receipts and/or Supplier’s Invoice, draw a Journal Voucher (JV) to record the Inventories in the Journal of Procurements Received and in the Inventory Ledger Cards. In case of the inventories acquired thru non-change transactions, the Report on Receipt of In-Kind Donations for Inventories shall be the basis for drawing the JV for the recognition of the Inventories in the books of accounts.
The Municipal General Services Officer submits to the Municipal Accountant the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI), together with the supporting RIS, within five (5) days after the end of the month. Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant shall use this as a basis in drawing a JV to record the issuances.
The Municipal General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant ensure compliance with the procedures for the preparation and maintenance of the various forms necessary to support, control and monitor the requisition, receipt and issuance of all inventory items, acquired either through procurement or donation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer review and reconcile their records and prepare the necessary and appropriate adjusting entries to ensure that the correct balances of Cash in Bank Local Currency Account for the Trust Fund-LDRRMF and Trust Liabilities-LDRRMF accounts are reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
facilitate the completion of the required documents/attachments of the payable accounts to support their balances in the FS and to present fairly the financial transactions of the LGU;
coordinate with the claimants to facilitate the settlement of the obligations of the Agency, especially to its identified/known officers and employees; and
for future transactions, ensure that claims/obligations against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation in compliance with Sec. 4 (6) of PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the Municipal General Services Officer, exert extra effort to locate the records relevant to the aforementioned PPE accounts as a basis for the provision of the corresponding depreciation and as such, prepare the necessary journal entries to present fairly the balances in the financial statements. Otherwise, the Management may opt to apply the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the “One-Time Cleansing” of PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Require the accountable officers/employees to liquidate/settle the long outstanding cash advances in compliance with COA Circular 97-002 and PD 1445. In addition, additional cash advances shall not be granted to any officials and employees unless the previous cash advance is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made.
Compel the Cooperative for the immediate submission of the fund utilization reports or any necessary documents to support the fund transfers in the total amount of P6,000,000.00, otherwise, necessitate the refund of the amount.

We recommended that the Management:
Hasten the process of identification of completed projects in order that the balances of fund transfers with completed purposes are returned immediately to the Source Agencies. Henceforth, comply with the provisions of COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 on the return of unutilized balances of fund transfers to Source Agencies as soon as the purposes of the fund transfers are completed.
Review and analyze the transactions to identify the cause of the negative balances and make the necessary adjustments to present the correct balance of the account.
Reclassify the balances of the received fund transfers from GOCC and other LGUs and the recording of retention fees.
Ensure submission of complete subsidiary ledgers/supporting schedules in compliance with Item 2.0 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

The Municipal GSO reconcile the Report on Physical Count of Inventories with that of the records maintained by the Municipal Accountant to ensure that appropriate amount of inventories are reported in the financial statements.
The Municipal GSO verify and include in the physical inventory count those inventory items, issued to other offices during the year, as applicable, but remained undistributed or unconsumed at year-end and the Municipal accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry to restore back those items to Inventory account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue the efforts in the thorough review of the journal entries made, identify the nature of the transactions causing the unreconciled balance, obtain the necessary supporting documents or reports and prepare the necessary and appropriate adjusting entries in order to present fairly the balances of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer make it a practice to reconcile their books of accounts at least quarterly.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that:
All accountable officers remit daily their corresponding collections, supported by Report on Collections and Deposits and complete official receipts; and
All collections/receipts be deposited daily, when applicable, to the authorized depository banks of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant monitor the implementation of the funds and require the submission of the liquidation reports by the recipients to ensure proper accountability of the Special Education Funds. We also recommended for the immediate liquidation of the previous years’ outstanding receivable.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer trace the unreconciled amounts in the Intra-Agency payable and receivable accounts and prepare the necessary and appropriate adjusting entries. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the reconciliation of the LGU records of the unremitted previous years’ balances with the records maintained by the BIR, GSIS, HDMF and PhilHealth.

We recommended that the Management follow the procedures and accounting policies in Sections 134 and 268 of the GAM for the loss of the Biological Assets and to properly effect the accounting entries in order to present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements.

The Budget Unit prepare and maintain the Registry of Cash Donations and Utilization; and
The Accounting Unit record the cash donations received in the “Trust Liabilities-DRRMF” account and prepare/maintain subsidiary ledger for cash donations received amounting to P100,000.00 and above per donor per purpose while those below P100,000.00 shall be grouped and posted in one SL labeled as various donors per purpose.

strictly observe the provisions of RA 9184, most importantly the prescribed period for the completion of the procurement activities and ensure compliance with the contract agreement in order to take advantage of the maximum benefits of the procurement at the earliest possible time.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the transactions in their appropriate expense or asset accounts in accordance with the LGU Chart of Accounts to present fairly the relevant accounts in the financial statements. We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that management exhaust all possible means to collect the advance payments totaling P1,293,488.55 from the contractors so that funds could be used to implement other priority development projects/programs/activities which are beneficial to the constituents of the local government unit.

We have recommended management the following:

The Local Chief Executive to direct the concerned officials to submit duly approved payroll indicating the names of the payees and the corresponding net payment before effecting the grant of cash advances for the payment of wages of Job Order and Contract of Service employees of the municipality.

Strictly adhere with the existing laws, rules and regulations covering the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Local Development Fund to avoid possible sanctions that may be imposed for non-compliance.

The Municipal Development Council specifically identify the projects for inclusion in the AIP to secure approval/authorization from local Sanggunian for the implementation of projects/programs/activities that were described in generic terms.

To regularly observe the submission of the PhilGEPS Registration Number issued to suppliers as an eligibility requirement in compliance with Section 54.6 of Republic Act No. 9184 in order to promote transparency in the procurement activities of the municipality under the alternative methods of procurement.

Deficiencies noted be given immediate action and relevant documents needed be timely submitted.
Strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of RA No. 9184 specifically Section 23 and 25.2 (a) and (b) and

For the HRMO to identify the specific duties and responsibilities as required under each department/ office to avoid duplication of work done by regular employees and to facilitate review of whether hiring of these personnel are necessary. In case of savings from the local budget, submit to the Audit Team copy of the resolution from the Sanggunian Bayan authorizing the payment of honoraria to BAC and TWG chargeable against the General Fund.
Non-compliance hereof may warrant issuance of the Notice of Disallowance pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

We have recommended that Management discontinue the funding for the Administrative Expenses of BAC under Special Purpose Appropriation and strictly adhere to DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-3 dated November 29, 2007 and GPPB Resolution No. 18-2012 dated July 27, 2012 to ensure that funds appropriated in the Annual Budget are with valid basis to avoid disallowance in audit.

That management may consider the acquisition of a biometric machine for the LGU personnel to register actual and prescribed office or work hours in order to ensure correctness in the computation/payment of leave credits, salaries and wages.

Require the Municipal Accountant to record any balances of donations received be costed and recorded in the books or in the registries.

Submit the documents being required to support the eligibility of the grantees of the cash/financial assistance given by the municipality to establish the validity, legitimacy and propriety of the transaction.

We have recommended the following:

We have recommended that management exert more effort to collect these RPT delinquencies and delinquent stallholders especially those that have been long overdue in order for the municipality to augment its income that could be used for other beneficial projects that would be beneficial to the constituents of the municipality.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations on remittance of GSIS premiums.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations on remittance of PhilHealth contributions.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing laws, rules and regulations on remittance of Pag-IBIG contributions.

We have recommended that the local government unit transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and the Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund” (account number 2-04-01-020)  in the Trust Fund book.
Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 prescribing accounting and reporting guidelines for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund is further recommended.

We recommended that the inventory Committee conduct annual physical inventory of all the PPE of the LGU and prepare properly and completely the corresponding RPCPPE. Likewise, the Accounting and General Services Offices should maintain Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, for all PPE accounts. Further, the Accounting and General Services Offices should reconcile their records to obtain the accurate value of the PPE and such be presented fairly in the financial statements. Lastly, the Municipal Accountant reconcile the variances noted on the aforementioned PPE accounts and correspondingly make the necessary adjustments to present fairly the balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the responsible officials to comply with the accounting and reporting requirements of COA Circular 2015-008 for the fair presentation of the Local Road Network account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the General Services Officer facilitate the preparation of documentary requirements to transfer the titles on the subject lots in the name of the LGU. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the Land and Land Rights, which were accounted in the books of accounts but not supported by TCTs and other legal documents. Lastly, we recommended that Management exert extra efforts to identify the existence, location and condition of the other lots, with a carrying amount P397,743.20 and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of Land account.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide yearly depreciation expense for the subject PPE accounts following the straight line depreciation method with 5% residual value.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant adjust the previous reconciling items in the books to present the correct balance of Cash in Bank-Local Currency account in the financial statements. Likewise, we recommended that the discrepancy noted in the closed bank account be reconciled and necessary adjusting entry be made. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all check disbursements should be recorded in the CKDJ at the time of issuance based on the RCI and that all checks outstanding for each month should be properly reflected in the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements as bank reconciling items. Furthermore, we recommended that the Management verify the various previous years’ service charges debited from the bank accounts of the Agency and determine those responsible for the incurrence of service charges for the issuance of checks without sufficient funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile with the Municipal Treasurer the discrepancy noted and consequently demand the immediate deposit of the undeposited collection, if any. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer make it a practice to reconcile their books of accounts at least quarterly.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that Accounts Payable are supported with complete documentation, properly certified/approved by concerned Heads of Offices and other sufficient documentary evidence to show that the subject goods/services were already delivered/rendered to qualify as valid claims against the LGU. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant support the settlement of the Accounts Payable in the amount of P1,126,594.49 and thereafter, adjust in the books the accounts that are already settled/paid.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant classify the current and non-current portions of the Loans Payable under the line item Financial Liabilities for the fair presentation of the account, in the Statement of Financial Position. This is to assist the Management in the decision-making process, as well as in the financial planning, particularly in setting up priorities for funding of payment of currently maturing obligations of the LGU.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Management: (a) Require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile its records with the AMWS and make the necessary adjusting entry to reflect the correct balance of the Other Receivable account in the FS; (b) Ensure that the AMWS is guided with clear policies and strategies if any, that will be strictly implemented to enhance its collection system; (c) Require the concerned officials to intensify their collection efforts to consumers on their unsettled bills and develop control measures to ensure that quality water is provided to the end users; and (d) Ensure that the collecting function is performed by bonded regular employees.

To further strengthen the monitoring of the fund transfer, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Cooperative to submit, within a definite period of time, the fund utilization reports or any necessary documents to support the fund transfers in the total amount of P11,000,000.00, otherwise, non-submission within the agreed time shall necessitate the refund of the amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Local Treasury Office to determine the details of the balances of the barangay shares from local taxes and to expedite its release to the intended Barangays. Also, the Municipal Treasurer should issue checks to the barangays on a quarterly basis for their share in the real property tax in consonance to the provisions of the Local Government Code. Lastly, share of the barangays in other taxes should also be given within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the affected expense accounts and accounts payable to fairly present such in the financial statements. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer send written notices to payees on the existence of the check and determine its status if eligible for replacement.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the verification of the existence of the dormant Receivable accounts and make the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts. Moreover, analyze the accounts that are dormant or long outstanding and may request for the write off of the dormant Accounts Receivable accounts as prescribed in COA Circular 2016-005 to present fairly the accounts in the financial statements.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and reconcile the subject accounts with the records maintained by GSIS, Philhealth and BIR.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the account Due to LGU (Provincial Government of Cagayan) and reconcile its record with the Province on the difference noted in the details and balances. Also, prepare an adjusting entry, if necessary.

We recommended that the Management formally file a request for relief from accountability in accordance with Section 378 of the Local Government Code and Section 73 of PD 1445. Moreover, after complying with the procedural and documentary requirements in the filing of Request for Relief from Accountability, as provided in the aforementioned laws, we recommended that the necessary adjusting entries shall be prepared depending on the decision on the request which shall be rendered by the Commission.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer at year-end the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liabilities-DRRMF” in the Trust Fund books pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Section 21 of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 10121.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that:
The MDRRMC prepare the LDRRMFIP by adopting the prescribed format in the Annex A of the COA Circular 2012-002.
The MDRRMC review and update annually the LDRRMFIP to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years, which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund.
The Municipal Accountant prepare and certify correct the monthly report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the prescribed format in Annex B of the Circular.
The MDRRO submit to the Auditor the report on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council (LDC)

We recommended the Management that the above early preparedness actions to reduce disaster risks as required by the above laws and regulations be included in the formulation and development of the DRRM Plan in the succeeding year.

We recommended that the Management consider augmenting the plantilla position of three (3) LDRRM staff thru the options provided in Item 5.5 of NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 on the Implementing guidelines for the Establishment of Local DRRM Offices in the LGU based on Section 12 of RA 10121. And if the LGU is financially capable and has not exceeded the Personal Services (PS) limitation, the Local Sanggunian, may elevate the organization and staffing of the LDRRM Office.

We recommended that the Management ensure submission of contracts within 5 days from the date of perfection to the Office of the Auditor for auditorial review in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Management timely submit the approved and endorsed GAD plan and strictly comply with the provisions of JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by JMC No. 2016-01, with regard to the preparation of the GAD Plan to ensure that the PPAs provided therein are responsive with identified GAD-related issues and concerns to ensure.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the submission of the complete accounts of the Agency within the prescribed period in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006. Moreover, the agency officials concerned should identify the reasons for the delayed submission of accounts and take necessary actions to address the same to ensure compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Management closely monitor and fast track the implementation of the planned projects that would contribute to the LGU’s attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.
We recommended that Management require the persons liable to settle fully their audit disallowances.

We have recommended that the Agency Head install, implement, and monitor a sound system of internal control in the operation of the Agro-Industrial Warehouse/Mechanical Drier to safeguard government resources against the risk of loss or misuse. For the foregoing deficiencies, we have recommended the following:

a. 	Instruct the person assigned to collect drying fees to refrain from issuing temporary receipts. Payment of drying fees be made directly to the Treasury Department to ensure prompt issuance of official receipts in compliance with Section 72, GAAM Volume I and Section 68 of PD 1445; 

b. 	Require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact full amounts so received and collected from the operation of the Agro-Industrial Warehouse/Mechanical Drier in compliance with Section 69 of PD 1445;

c. 	Assign representatives from the LGU Departments especially from the Treasury Department who will serve as witnesses in weighing wet corn seeds subject to drying. This is to ensure that there is oversight and review to check errors and minimize the chances if not eliminate occurrence of fraud or theft. 

Further, require the use of truck scale to determine with accuracy the kilos of wet corn seeds weighed which will serve as the basis in computing the amount of drying fees to be collected.

d. 	Require the clients to present proof of payment before drying the wet corn seeds to ensure collection of income and avoid encumbrances of collecting receivables; and

e. 	Utilize only regular and bonded revenue collectors to undertake the collection of drying fees.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Accounting Unit record the purchase of all supplies and materials as inventory following the perpetual inventory method. Issuances thereof should be recorded as they take place using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) which shall be submitted by the General Service Officer or Local Treasurer as the case maybe.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that claims of Representation and Transportation Allowances be supported with the required documents in order for us to have basis in determining whether the provisions of the aforecited laws, rules and regulations were observed in granting RATA. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer should see to it that the claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment to avoid suspension and/or disallowance of the transactions.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 92-389 to maintain a sound internal control in the disbursement of government funds.

We have further recommended that the disbursement vouchers and their supporting documents be marked “PAID” after payment in order to avoid the risk of double payment or reuse of supporting documents.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned personnel to submit the copies of perfected contracts to the Audit Team within five working days as prescribed in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 in order to allow the Audit Team to perform effective review and generate timely and relevant audit results. Administrative disciplinary action as provided in Section 4 of the same circular against the concerned employee be imposed upon unjustified failure of compliance.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the concerned personnel exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays that preclude the timely conduct of audit and rendering of the required reports in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the local government unit refrain from allowing deductions from salaries and other benefits accruing to their employees which shall reduce the employee’s monthly net take home pay to an amount lower than Four Thousand Pesos in compliance with Section 47 of Republic Act No. 10924 also known as General Appropriation Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2017.

We have recommended that management release the share of the barangays from the basic real property tax earnings on a quarterly basis within five days after the end of each quarter in accordance with the provision of Section 271 of Republic Act 7160 also known and cited as the Local Government Code of 1991 to enable the barangays to use the additional income for development projects which are beneficial to its constituents.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the local government unit procure its common-use supplies directly from the Procurement Service or its depots in compliance with the provisions of Section 20 of GAA 2017 and Sections 1 and 4 of Administrative Order No. 17 dated July 28, 2011 in order to contribute in the attainment of goals and objectives in modernizing and standardizing government procurement system.

We have recommended that management be more prudent in purchasing mobile phones which should be essential and responsive to the exigencies of the service. We have further recommended that management strictly comply with the guidelines on the prevention of unnecessary expenditures as provided under COA Circular No. 2012-003 to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the local government unit exert all effort to ensure the sustainability of the organic production project through a repayment or roll-over scheme for the next in line beneficiaries in compliance with the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement and see to it that the constituents are not deprived of the essential benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We have recommended that management ensure that the projects/programs/activities designed under the 20% Development Fund are implemented within the timeframe. Optimal utilization of the foregoing fund was also recommended in order to deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes to the constituents and address the needs of the poor constituents and marginalized sectors in the municipality towards the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and remedies provided in Section 254 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the “Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes and employ all necessary measures to enable collection of unpaid stall rentals.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the GSIS regulations remittance of monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of Pag-IBIG premiums.

We have recommended that the local government unit continue to adhere with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of PhilHealth premiums.

While we have commended the local government unit for allocating at least five percent (5%) of the total LGU Budget for GAD PPAs and prepare GAD Plan and Budget, we have recommended that the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report be submitted to the COA Audit Team within the prescribed period as required under Item V of COA Circular No. 2014 – 001.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairman of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), appropriate one percent (1%) of the Internal Revenue Allotment for the strengthening and implementation of the programs, projects and activities of the LCPC in compliance with the provisions of Section 15 of Republic Act 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.

We have further recommended that all members of the council be encouraged to participate in the preparation of Local Development Plan and Local Investment Plan for Children and other reports in order to have more accurate basis in determining the needs of children and other children related issues that needs immediate attention and action.

We have recommended that the local government unit transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and the Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund” (account number 2-04-01-020)  in the Trust Fund book.
Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 prescribing accounting and reporting guidelines for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund was further recommended.

observations.managementAction: A committee was created to take charge in the physical inventory of PPE as well as the preparation of Report of Physical Inventory.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the local government unit ensure that all of its insurable assets, properties and interests are insured with the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System in compliance with Republic Act No. 656, otherwise known as the “Property Insurance Law”, as amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 dated July 13, 1973 and Administrative Order No. 33 dated August 25, 1987 by identifying all its insurable assets, submitting an inventory report of insurable assets to the System, setting aside in its appropriation the amount needed for premiums and remitting the premiums to the System within the prescribed period to avoid unnecessary losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, assets and properties brought by fortuitous events and guarantee immediate repair or replacement and uninterrupted delivery of public service.

We recommended that management stop the practice of paying cash advance without legitimate or valid purpose.  The Municipal Accountant should not sign/certify the disbursement vouchers if not supported with complete documentation to ensure proper utilization of funds.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the local government unit ensure that all of its insurable assets, properties and interests are insured with the General Insurance Fund (GIF) administered by the Government Service Insurance System in compliance with Republic Act No. 656, otherwise known as the “Property Insurance Law”, as amended by Presidential Decree No. 245 dated July 13, 1973 and Administrative Order No. 33 dated August 25, 1987 by identifying all its insurable assets, submitting an inventory report of insurable assets to the System, setting aside in its  appropriation the amount needed for premiums and remitting the premiums to the System within the prescribed period to avoid unnecessary losses in the event of damage to, or loss of, assets and properties brought by fortuitous events and guarantee immediate repair or replacement and uninterrupted delivery of public service.

We have recommended that the local government unit seek legal assistance from Provincial Legal Office or Prosecutor’s Office in compliance with the provisions of Section 481 of RA 7160 and Revised Guidelines on the Deputation of Private Lawyers and Special Attorneys prescribed in COA Memorandum No. 2016-010 dated December 15, 2015.  We have further recommended that the LGU refrain from using government funds to pay the services of private lawyer to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that management exhaust all possible means to collect the advance payments totaling P1,593,488.55 from the contractors so that funds could    be used to implement other priority development projects/programs/activities which are beneficial to the constituents of the local government unit.

We have recommended that management refrain from using government funds to defray expenses for religious purposes and ensure that the funds would instead be used in implementing programs/projects/activities that would benefit its    constituents. Strict compliance with the provision of Section 335 of the Local Government Code of 1991 was further recommended to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that management strictly comply with the guidelines on the disbursements related to cultural and athletic activities as prescribed in Section 48  of RA 10717 also known as General Appropriations Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2016 and Section 10 of COA Circular 2012-001 to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the Head of the Local School Board, adhere strictly with the existing laws, rules and regulations in the prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund particularly for the  operation and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings, facilities and equipment, educational research, purchase of books and periodicals and sports development as provided in Sections 100 (c) and 272 of   the Local Government Code of 1991 and DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circulars in order to ensure an effective educational development within the locality.

We have recommended that management strictly adhere with the guidelines relative to the monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts to ensure that these are  collected when become due and demandable. Strict compliance with the procedures  in the write-off of dormant accounts as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2016-005   was further recommended to avoid overstatement of agency assets due to inclusion   of accounts with doubtful existence.

We have recommended that the local government unit adhere with the existing guidelines on the manual labor component of projects undertaken by administration and submit supporting documents showing compliance thereof.

We have reiterated our previous year recommendation that the procurement of  goods which are similar in nature be consolidated and procured through public bidding. The Bids and Awards Committee, who has the responsibility for ensuring that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by RA 9184 and its IRR, should strictly adhere with all provisions of law, rules and regulations pertaining to procurements to protect at all times the interest of the local government unit.

We have reiterated our previous year recommendation that the Accounting Unit record the purchase of all supplies and materials as inventory following the perpetual inventory method. Issuances thereof should be recorded as they take place using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) which shall be submitted by the General Service Officer or Local Treasurer as the case maybe.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the primarily responsible  in the management of government resources, require the concerned personnel to comply with the required submission of pertinent documents and such other reports as may be necessary in the exercise of our audit functions for the effective review of financial transactions and ascertaining compliance with laws, rules and regulations.

We have recommended that claims of Representation and Transportation Allowances be supported with the required documents in order for us to have basis in determining whether the provisions of the aforecited laws, rules and regulations were observed in granting RATA. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer should see to it that the claims for RATA are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment to avoid suspension and/or disallowance of the transactions.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the concerned personnel exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays that preclude the timely conduct of audit and rendering of the required reports in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

We have recommended that the local government unit sustain the operation of the Material Recovery Facility in an environmentally sound manner and require the establishment of MRF in every barangay or cluster barangays in compliance with   the provisions of RA 9003 or the Ecological Solid Waste Management Program to protect the constituents against human health and environment hazards.

We have recommended that the agency procure its common-use supplies directly from the Procurement Service or its depots in compliance with provisions of Section 21 of GAA 2016 and Sections 1 and 4 of Administrative Order No. 17 dated July 28, 2011 to support the national government attain its goal and objectives in modernizing and standardizing government procurement systems.

We have recommended that the local government unit conduct market survey or research relative to the procurement at hand as prescribed in Volume II of GPPB Manual of Procedures for the Procurement of Goods and Services to evaluate the needs of the intended beneficiaries. The LGU should also see to it that the fund from Tobacco Excise Tax be utilized in pursuit of the objectives provided under Section   4.2 of Local Budget Memorandum No. 71 dated September 21, 2015 and ensure that funds for programs/projects/activities geared toward the welfare of its constituents would not be sacrificed.

We have recommended that the local government unit exert all effort to ensure the sustainability of the organic production project through a repayment or roll-over scheme for the next in line beneficiaries in compliance with the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement and see to it that the constituents are not deprived of the essential benefits that could be derived therefrom to promote efficient and effective utilization of government resources.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the concerned LGU officials and employees exert extra effortto intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt the procedures and remedies provided in Section 254 and 256 of Republic Act No. 7160, otherwise known as the “Local Government Code of 1991, to enable the collections of unpaid local taxes and employ all necessary measures to enable collection of unpaid stall rentals.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the LGU which may be beneficial to its constituents.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the existing rules and regulations concerning the remittance of taxes withheld.
We have recommended that the local government unit continue to comply with the GSIS regulations remittance of monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share.

We have commended the local government unit for allocating at least five percent (5%) of the total LGU Budget for GAD PPAs and prepare GAD Plan and Budget. On the other hand, we have recommended that the GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report be submitted to the COA Audit Team within the prescribed period as required under Item V of COA Circular No. 2014 – 001.

We have recommended that the local government unit transfer all unexpended/unobligated balance of the Quick Response Fund and the Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund” (account number 2-04-01-020)  in the Trust Fund book.
Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 prescribing accounting and reporting guidelines for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund was further recommended.

DRRM Funds to Trust Funds					x x x
Cash
To recognize transfer of unexpended/unobligated balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE to special trust fund account.

Cash						x x x
Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction
and Management Fund					x x x
To recognize transfer of unexpended/unobligated balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE from General Fund.
We have further recommended that the local government unit require the Municipal Accountant and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO) to prepare and submit the monthly sources and utilization of the LDRRMF in compliance with the Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that management conduct physical count of all properties found in station and submit the corresponding Report of Physical Count of PPE, same will be reconciled with the accounting records.
We further recommended that management suspend the salary of the person responsible in case of failure to prepare and submit the required reports without justifiable reason, until complied with pursuant to Section 122 of Presidential Decree No. 1445. Management should likewise initiate disciplinary action against person responsible for negligence in the discharge of their official duties.

We recommended that management instruct the Accounting Office to grant cash advance equal to the total net amount in the payroll pursuant to Section 4.2.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002. Further, require the concerned officials to liquidate immediately the amounts or refund the excess amount in order to relieve them from accountability.
Otherwise, failure to liquidate or refund the outstanding cash shall constitute cause of filing of malversation charge against them pursuant to Article 217 and Article 218 both under the Revised Penal Code or criminal prosecution under Section 128 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the Accounting Office should not certify disbursement vouchers or grant subsequent cash advance without requiring submission of liquidation reports by the concerned official. Failure to liquidate cash advances shall constitute cause of filing of malversation charge under Article 217 and Article 218 both of the Revised Penal Code or criminal prosecution under Section 128 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the accountant stop the practice of granting cash advance to elective officials other than their travelling expenses pursuant to Section 4.1.1.4 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
Further, under no circumstance that an additional cash advances be granted to any official or employee unless previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made.

We recommended that management require the accountant to send demand letters to all persons concerned, if no  liquidation reports are submitted within the prescribed period cause the withholding of their salaries pursuant to paragraph 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and the institution of necessary sanction as provided under COA Circular 2012-004.

We recommended that the agency accountant should not allow payments of repairs and maintenance without complying with the submission of the supporting documents required under Section 9.1.3.4 of Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure veracity, regularity of the expenses incurred.

We recommended that management maintain a separate financial statement/subsidiary ledgers for the special accounts in the General Fund Proper to ensure adequate information of the individual  special account and proper accounting thereof.

We recommended that management include in their budget the creation of the Local DRRM Officer position to ensure efficient and effective implementation of disaster management programs and strengthen the LGU capacity in the conduct of risk reduction activities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to submit the official receipts, paid disbursement vouchers and payrolls and their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within ten (10) day  from receipt of said documents as required under paragraph 6.05 of  Circular No. 95-006 dated  to facilitate the review and audit of the accounts.
Otherwise, suspend the salary of the Accountant/person responsible for failure to submit the required reports without justifiable reason,   until complied with the requirements pursuant to Section 122 of Presidential Decree 1445.

It was recommended that the Municipal Treasurer  in coordination with the Municipal Assessor prepare and furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in the setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes at the beginning of the year for fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

It was recommended that management request for write-off of the dormant receivable accounts from the COA undertaking the following procedures:

The Municipal Accountant should prepare a recommendation addressed to the Local Chief Executive supported with the documents provided by COA Circular No. 97-001.

Upon approval of the recommendation, the Local Chief Executive shall forward the approved recommendation for write-off of dormant receivable accounts to the Sangguniang Bayan for the adoption of a resolution.

The Sangguniang Bayan shall forward the duly approved SB Resolution and supporting documents to the Local Chief Executive.

The Local Chief Executive  shall prepare the request for write-off based on the duly signed SB Resolution and supporting documents and forwards the same to the Commission on Audit for review and for approval.

It was recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Engineer to use five percent of the sum of the Estimated Direct Cost, Overhead, Contingencies and Miscellaneous (OCM) and Contractor’s Profit (CP) as VAT component in the computation of indirect costs of infrastructure projects in compliance with the provisions of Department Order No. 22 dated February 18, 2015 of DPWH.

Since the Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) is the ceiling for acceptable bid prices, we have further recommended that the guidelines provided in the same Department Order of DPWH in the preparation of ABC be strictly observed.

It was recommended that management strictly observe the limitations on payment of honoraria of the chair and members of Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), Technical Working Group and BAC Secretariat provided in Sections 15 of the Republic Act No. 9184 and Section 5 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-5A in order to avoid excessive payment of  BAC Honoraria. The excess honoraria received by the concerned chair and members of the Bids and Awards Committee, Technical Working Group and BAC Secretariat be refunded immediately. 

We have also reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the honoraria be charged only against the sources enumerated in Section 3.1 and that the provisions of Sections 3.3 and 3.5 of Budget Circular No. 2007-3 dated November 29, 2007 be strictly observed in the use of savings for payment of honoraria.

It was recommended that management strictly observe the existing laws, rules regulations and guidelines in the grant of compensation for the procurement services rendered by the members of the Bids and Awards Committee, the Technical Working Group (TWG) and the Secretariat. In view of the absence of legal basis, we have further recommended that management cause the refund of the load allowance received by concerned members of the Bids and Awards Committee, TWG, Secretariat, NGO  Observer and support staff.

It was recommended that management require the concerned officials and employees to submit the required documents so as to avoid suspension in audit. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer see to it that the supporting documents are complete before certifying and effecting payment. 

Strict compliance with the provisions of the law, rules and regulations pertaining to documentation of  claims against government funds is further recommended.
